top_stories.label: Top Stories
top_stories.ai_summary: The evening is defined by a Middle East ceasefire unravelling almost as fast as it was struck. Hezbollah's leader rejected the US-brokered Israel–Lebanon deal hours after it was announced, and Israel vowed to keep striking the south. Washington and Tehran, meanwhile, traded clashing signals over their own truce, and Israeli strikes killed at least eight in Gaza as disarmament talks stalled. Away from the conflicts, South Korea's ruling party swept nationwide local elections but lost the symbolic prize of Seoul.

stories.headline: Hezbollah rejects US-brokered Israel–Lebanon ceasefire as Israel vows to keep striking the south
stories.summary: Hezbollah Secretary-General Naim Qassem publicly rejected the US-brokered Israel–Lebanon ceasefire on 4 June, a day after Israel and Lebanon agreed to it in Washington without Hezbollah, the main fighting party. In a statement read on Al-Manar TV, Qassem said demands for fighters to leave southern Lebanon 'under fire' would mean 'surrender, defeat and achieving the enemy's goals,' and that the group would keep striking northern Israel while attacks continue. Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz said the military would not withdraw and would keep operating, including in Beirut. The deal sets up Lebanese-Armed-Forces 'pilot zones' in the south and is contingent on Hezbollah withdrawing north of the Litani, with talks set to resume the week of 22 June (statements attributed to the parties; not independently verified).
stories.source: Times of Israel / Washington Post / Al Jazeera

stories.headline: US and Iran trade contradictory signals on their truce as Gulf strikes reverberate
stories.summary: Washington and Tehran sent clashing messages on 4 June about their fragile, April-brokered ceasefire. President Trump said a deal to extend the truce and reopen the Strait of Hormuz could come 'over the weekend,' while Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi told Al Mayadeen there was 'no formal negotiation process underway,' only an exchange of messages, and insisted the war ends only 'when it also ends in Lebanon' — a linkage US officials reject. The exchange followed strikes on 3 June in which a drone-and-missile attack damaged Kuwait International Airport, killing one and wounding 63-plus (Kuwaiti figures), and US Central Command said it hit an Iranian control station on Qeshm Island (claims attributed to the parties; not independently verified).

stories.headline: Israeli strikes kill at least eight in Gaza as Hamas disarmament talks stall
stories.summary: Gaza's civil defence agency said Israeli strikes killed at least eight people on 4 June — seven in residential buildings and one in the Al-Shati refugee camp west of Gaza City — with Al-Shifa Hospital reporting 15 wounded. Despite a truce nominally in force since October 2025, Gaza's health ministry says Israel has killed more than 930 people since the ceasefire began. The figures, attributed to Gaza authorities, are not independently verified and do not distinguish civilians from combatants. Diplomacy remained deadlocked after talks between Hamas and Board of Peace envoy Nickolay Mladenov were postponed on 3 June over Hamas disarmament, which mediators call 'not negotiable.'

stories.headline: South Korea's ruling party sweeps local elections but loses Seoul
stories.summary: South Korea's ruling Democratic Party won a landslide in nationwide local elections held on 3 June, taking 12 of 16 metropolitan mayor and governor races, including Busan, the Japan Times and other outlets reported. The opposition People Power Party held four seats, with conservative incumbent Oh Se-hoon winning another term as mayor of the capital, Seoul — a symbolic setback for President Lee's party in the country's most politically important city. Analysts linked Seoul's result partly to discontent over the government's housing policy, even as strong exports buoyed the ruling party nationally (seat tallies as reported; not an official final count).
stories.source: The Japan Times / Yahoo News / Xinhua

singapore.label: Singapore
singapore.ai_summary: A fairly quiet local evening. The National Environment Agency's 5.31pm forecast points to partly cloudy skies, a high near 34°C and a lightning risk if showers develop. June's first COE bidding closed at 4pm with mixed results — premiums for smaller cars (Cat A) edged up to about S$126,000 while larger-car (Cat B) prices slipped. In business, US AI firm Anthropic — maker of the Claude chatbot — is moving to set up a Singapore office, while a viral SAFRA Jurong parking row has cost a visiting Hong Kong principal his job.

stories.headline: NEA forecasts partly cloudy skies, highs near 34°C and a lightning risk
stories.summary: The National Environment Agency's 24-hour forecast, updated at 5.31pm on 4 June, expects partly cloudy conditions with temperatures ranging from a low of about 26°C to a high near 34°C and winds from the south-southeast at 10–25 km/h. NEA flagged an imminent lightning risk during any thundery showers and advised the public to take shelter when storms approach.

stories.headline: COE premiums end mixed in June's first bidding exercise; Cat A rises, Cat B falls
stories.summary: The first Certificate of Entitlement bidding exercise for June 2026 closed at 4pm on 4 June with mixed results, per the Land Transport Authority's published results. Category A premiums (smaller cars) rose about S$1,780 to S$126,009, while Category B (larger cars) fell roughly S$2,512 to S$126,989. Category C (goods vehicles and buses) climbed to around S$94,000, Category D (motorcycles) edged up to about S$10,000, and the Open category (Cat E) eased to around S$129,000 (figures as reported from LTA's bidding results).

stories.headline: Anthropic, maker of the Claude AI chatbot, moves to set up Singapore operations
stories.summary: Anthropic, the US AI company behind the Claude chatbot, is advertising several Singapore-based roles, signalling plans to establish operations in the city-state. The positions span finance, product support and economic research, including an Asia-Pacific accounting lead, product-support specialists, and a regional research economist; the economist post lists an annual salary of S$307,200 to S$331,200, while pay for the other roles was not disclosed. The move adds to Singapore's growing cluster of AI labs; Singapore's GIC is among Anthropic's backers.

stories.headline: Hong Kong school fires principal filmed in SAFRA Jurong parking dispute during Singapore trip
stories.summary: A Hong Kong secondary school dismissed its principal, Lee Cheuk-hing, over a confrontation with security guards at SAFRA Jurong during a Singapore study tour on 22 May, when the group's bus was parked on double yellow lines and blocked access. Lee was filmed arguing with two female guards and swearing in Cantonese, in a clip that went viral in both cities. He had apologised and tendered his resignation, but the management committee of San Wui Commercial Society Secondary School in Tuen Mun rejected it and fired him immediately, saying his continued presence would gravely disrupt the school.

wars.label: Wars & Conflicts
wars.ai_summary: Israel is escalating on two fronts even as diplomacy stirs. It renewed a US-brokered Lebanon ceasefire without Hezbollah, whose leader rejected it on 4 June as 'surrender' while Israel's defence minister vowed to keep striking south of the Litani; Israeli strikes also killed at least eight in Gaza, per civil defence. Russia and Ukraine traded their heaviest blows in months — a 73-missile, 656-drone barrage answered by deep drone strikes on St Petersburg — with no talks. US–Iran fighting flared after the Kuwait airport strike, though Trump claims a Hormuz deal is near. All casualty and strike counts are attributed claims by parties to the conflicts.

theatres.name: Russia–Ukraine War
theatres.status: The air war is at its most intense in months, with no ceasefire talks underway. Ukraine's Air Force said Russia launched 73 missiles and 656 drones overnight into 2 June; Ukrainian authorities reported at least 22 killed and roughly 130–138 wounded, mostly in Kyiv and Dnipro, while Russia's Defence Ministry said it struck only military-industrial, fuel and transport targets. Ukraine retaliated on 3 June with long-range drones that set a St Petersburg oil terminal ablaze as Russia opened its flagship economic forum, and said it also hit the Kronstadt naval base — claiming a strike on the corvette Boikiy; Russia said it downed 354 drones. Strikes continued into 4 June as Zelensky pressed for more air defences (Ukrainian and Russian official accounts; not independently verified).

timeline.event: Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine, beginning the largest land war in Europe since World War II.

Russia struck Kyiv in one of its heaviest bombardments of the war; Ukraine said Russia used a hypersonic Oreshnik missile and reported deaths and dozens wounded, with Zelensky calling for 'consequences' (Ukrainian figures).

Ukraine's Air Force said Russia launched 73 missiles and 656 drones overnight; Ukrainian officials reported at least 22 killed (including children in Kyiv) and about 130–138 wounded, and said air defences destroyed or suppressed 40 missiles and 602 drones. Russia's Defence Ministry said it targeted military-industrial, fuel and transport facilities (Ukrainian and Russian figures).

Zelensky said Ukrainian drones flew over 1,000 km to strike and ignite a St Petersburg oil terminal as Russia's economic forum opened, and that Ukraine also hit the Kronstadt naval base — claiming a strike on the corvette Boikiy — and a weapons plant in Tambov. Russia's Defence Ministry said air defences downed 354 drones; Pulkovo airport diverted flights (Ukrainian and Russian official accounts; not independently verified).

Russian strikes on Ukraine continued; Zelensky reiterated that US Patriot supplies are 'absolutely necessary' and urged Europe to build its own anti-ballistic defences. No ceasefire negotiations were underway.

theatres.name: US/Israel–Iran (Strait of Hormuz)
theatres.status: The fragile, April-brokered US–Iran ceasefire was tested again as fighting flared around the Strait of Hormuz and the two sides traded contradictory signals on diplomacy. Kuwait said Iranian drones and missiles struck Kuwait International Airport on 3 June, killing one (an Indian national) and wounding at least 63, with its defence ministry reporting about 30 incoming Iranian missiles and drones; the IRGC said it had targeted US sites in Kuwait and Bahrain in retaliation for US strikes near the strait, but later denied hitting the terminal, blaming a misfired US Patriot — a claim Kuwait and US Central Command rejected. The US said it struck an Iranian ground-control station on Qeshm Island. On 4 June, Trump said a deal to reopen the strait and extend the truce was reachable 'over the weekend,' while Iran's FM Araghchi said there is 'no formal negotiation process underway' (claims attributed to the parties; not independently verified).

timeline.event: The US and Israel launched coordinated airstrikes on Iranian military, nuclear and leadership targets; Iran retaliated with missiles and drones on Israeli cities and US Gulf bases, triggering the wider war and the Strait of Hormuz shipping crisis (accounts attributed to the parties).

The US and Iran agreed to a conditional ceasefire that also covered Israel, but the Strait of Hormuz remained effectively closed and a US–Iran blockade standoff continued, leaving tankers stranded in the Gulf.

Kuwait said Iranian drones and missiles hit Kuwait International Airport, killing one (an Indian national) and wounding 63+, and that it detected about 30 Iranian missiles and drones. The IRGC said it targeted US bases in Kuwait and Bahrain in retaliation for US strikes near Hormuz, then denied hitting the airport terminal and blamed a misfired US Patriot; Kuwait and CENTCOM rejected the claim. The US said it struck an Iranian ground-control station on Qeshm Island (claims attributed to the parties; not independently verified).

Trump said a deal to extend the ceasefire and reopen the Strait of Hormuz could come 'over the weekend,' while Iran's FM Araghchi told Al Mayadeen there was 'no formal negotiation process underway' — only an exchange of messages — and that the war ends only when it also ends in Lebanon, a linkage US officials reject (statements attributed to the parties).

theatres.name: Israel–Lebanon (Hezbollah)
theatres.status: A US-brokered Israel–Lebanon ceasefire renewed in Washington on 3 June — featuring Lebanese-Armed-Forces 'pilot zones' in the south and contingent on Hezbollah withdrawing north of the Litani — was openly rejected by Hezbollah on 4 June, leaving it in serious doubt. Secretary-General Naim Qassem said on Al-Manar TV that pulling fighters out 'under fire' would be 'surrender, defeat,' and that Hezbollah will keep striking northern Israel while attacks continue. Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz said the military will not withdraw and will keep operating, including in Beirut. The deal was struck without Hezbollah, the main fighting party, and Lebanon's health ministry said Israeli strikes in the south killed at least eight on 3 June (casualty figures attributed to Lebanese authorities; not independently verified).

timeline.event: A US-brokered Israel–Lebanon ceasefire took effect and was later extended, but Hezbollah disarmament remained unresolved and both sides traded blame for violations.

Israeli forces captured the strategic 12th-century Beaufort Castle near Nabatieh, overlooking much of southern Lebanon, as Netanyahu ordered forces to push beyond the Litani River despite the earlier ceasefire.

Israel and Lebanon agreed in Washington to implement a ceasefire and create LAF-controlled 'pilot zones' in the south, contingent on Hezbollah ceasing fire and withdrawing north of the Litani, with talks set to reconvene the week of 22 June — but without Hezbollah's participation. Lebanon's health ministry said Israeli strikes in the south killed at least eight that day (casualty figures attributed to Lebanese authorities; not independently verified).

Hezbollah's Naim Qassem rejected the deal on Al-Manar TV, saying the group will not retreat from southern Lebanon and will keep striking northern Israel while attacks continue, calling withdrawal under fire 'surrender, defeat.' Israeli DM Israel Katz said the IDF will continue operations in Lebanon and will not withdraw, retaining freedom to strike including in Beirut (statements attributed to the parties; not independently verified).

theatres.name: Israel–Gaza
theatres.status: The Gaza ceasefire that took effect in October 2025 remains technically in force but badly frayed. Gaza's civil defence said Israeli strikes killed at least eight people on 4 June — seven in residential buildings and one in the Al-Shati refugee camp — with Al-Shifa Hospital reporting 15 wounded; Gaza's health ministry says Israel has killed more than 930 people since the truce began. The figures, attributed to Gaza authorities, are unverified and do not distinguish civilians from combatants. The phased peace plan is stalled over Hamas disarmament: talks between Hamas and Board of Peace envoy Nickolay Mladenov were postponed on 3 June, with mediators calling disarmament 'not negotiable' and Hamas tying it to a path toward Palestinian statehood while blaming Israel for truce violations.

timeline.event: A Gaza ceasefire took effect under a 20-point peace plan, halting major fighting and enabling a hostage exchange and a surge in humanitarian aid; phase two was announced on 14 January 2026 but later stalled.

An Israeli strike in Gaza City killed Mohammed Odeh, described as the leader of Hamas's military wing, along with several others; Israel called Odeh an 'architect' of the 7 October 2023 attack and Hamas confirmed his death (Israel says it targeted Hamas figures; Palestinian officials said civilians were among the dead).

Talks between Board of Peace envoy Nickolay Mladenov and Hamas were postponed to give mediators time to press Hamas to modify its stance on disarmament, which mediators call 'not negotiable'; Hamas blamed Israel for continued truce violations (statements attributed to the parties).

Gaza's civil defence said Israeli strikes killed at least eight — seven in residential buildings and one in the Al-Shati camp — with Al-Shifa Hospital reporting 15 wounded; Gaza's health ministry put the post-ceasefire death toll above 930. Figures are unverified (casualty figures attributed to Gaza authorities).

outbreaks.label: Outbreaks
outbreaks.ai_summary: Three outbreaks are active and the picture is unchanged since this afternoon — no new authoritative update has been published. The DRC–Uganda Bundibugyo Ebola PHEIC dominates: roughly 359–379 confirmed cases and about 61–63 deaths (agency tallies diverge), still spreading with only ~45% of contacts traced and no licensed vaccine — the gravest threat. A cruise-linked Andes hantavirus cluster (13 cases, 3 deaths) looks contained but keeps 600+ global contacts under watch, and US measles stands at 1,983 cases with no deaths. Direct risk to readers outside affected areas stays low.

outbreaks.name: DRC/Uganda Bundibugyo Ebola (PHEIC)
outbreaks.status: Active PHEIC, still escalating; confirmed cases climbing while suspected counts were revised down after testing. Contact tracing critically low (~45% vs >90% target). No licensed Bundibugyo-specific vaccine or treatment.
outbreaks.scope_note: Laboratory-confirmed, DRC + Uganda combined. Agency tallies diverge: the WHO Director-General (3 June briefing, DRC health-ministry figures as of 1 June) cited DRC 344 confirmed / 60 deaths plus Uganda 15 / 1 — about 359 / 61; the US CDC situation summary (as of 2 June) lists DRC 363 / 62 plus Uganda 15 / 1 (and one probable case/death) — about 379 / 63. WHO's formal validated line list (DON605, 29 May) is far lower at ~134 confirmed / 18 deaths, reflecting lab-validation lag and DRC's 29 May case-definition cleanup that removed ruled-out cases. DRC suspected cases fell to 116 (from >1,000 a week earlier). Ituri is the epicentre (~322 confirmed); North Kivu and South Kivu also affected. One US citizen infected in DRC is counted separately and treated in Germany. WHO risk: very high national, high regional, low global.

outbreaks.name: MV Hondius Andes hantavirus cluster (multi-country)
outbreaks.status: Largely contained; no new cases reported since late May, with multinational contact monitoring continuing through the long incubation window.
outbreaks.scope_note: Cases linked to the cruise ship MV Hondius: 11 confirmed + 2 probable = 13, with 3 deaths (2 confirmed, 1 probable), per WHO Disease Outbreak News DON604 (published 28 May, data as of 27 May), matching ECDC. Confirmed cases reported in 4 countries; more than 600 contacts (about 53% high-risk) across 32 countries/territories are under monitoring but are NOT counted as cases. Andes virus is the only hantavirus with documented person-to-person spread; WHO rates the global risk low. No newer DON as of 4 June.

outbreaks.name: United States measles
outbreaks.status: Elevated, outbreak-driven and near-record for the year; no deaths reported in 2026, with elimination status at risk.
outbreaks.scope_note: Confirmed US measles cases in 2026 across 40 jurisdictions, per the US CDC as of 28 May (1,974 among US residents plus 9 among international visitors); 0 deaths reported in 2026. About 93% are outbreak-associated across 30 outbreaks this year. US-only figure; CDC's weekly refresh (typically Wednesdays) was not yet posted as of 4 June, so the count is about a week old. Part of a wider global measles resurgence.

stories.headline: WHO warns of 'catastrophic collision of disease and conflict' as DRC Ebola passes 340 confirmed cases
stories.summary: At a 3 June media briefing after visiting the DRC, the WHO Director-General said the Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak had reached 344 confirmed cases and 60 deaths across 24 health zones in three provinces (DRC health-ministry figures as of 1 June), with Uganda at 15 confirmed cases and one death. He warned of a 'catastrophic collision of disease and conflict,' with insecurity making it nearly impossible to trace contacts — only about 45% are being followed up, well below the 90%+ needed. Suspected cases in DRC fell to 116 from more than 1,000 the previous week as testing ruled cases out. There is still no licensed vaccine or treatment for this Ebola species.

stories.headline: US CDC tally puts DRC Ebola higher than WHO's validated line list as agencies diverge
stories.summary: The US CDC's Ebola situation summary (as of 2 June) reports 363 confirmed cases and 62 deaths in the DRC, plus 15 confirmed cases and one death (and one probable) in Uganda — higher than the WHO Director-General's 1 June figures of 344 DRC confirmed. CDC notes the DRC updated its case-counting methodology on 29 May to remove cases ruled out through investigation and testing, and that the situation is evolving rapidly. The gap between CDC's real-time tally, the WHO DG briefing and WHO's validated line list reflects lab-validation and reporting lags rather than a separate outbreak. CDC says one US citizen exposed while caring for patients in the DRC tested positive and was moved to Germany in stable condition.

stories.headline: Cruise-ship Andes hantavirus cluster holds at 13 cases, 3 deaths as contacts are traced across 32 countries
stories.summary: WHO's Disease Outbreak News DON604 reports the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus cluster at 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) and 3 deaths as of 27 May, with confirmed cases in four countries. All passengers have disembarked, and more than 600 contacts — about 53% of them high-risk — are being monitored across 32 countries and territories given the virus's long incubation period. Andes virus is the only hantavirus known to spread person-to-person; WHO rates the global risk low. No newer DON has been issued as of 4 June.

stories.headline: US measles tops 1,980 cases for 2026, almost all outbreak-linked
stories.summary: CDC data as of 28 May show 1,983 confirmed US measles cases in 2026 across 40 jurisdictions and 30 outbreaks, with about 93% of cases outbreak-associated and no deaths reported this year. Officials warn the sustained, interconnected transmission — among the highest annual totals since US elimination in 2000 — threatens the country's measles elimination status, amid a wider global resurgence.

cybersecurity.label: CyberSecurity
cybersecurity.ai_summary: Exploitable enterprise flaws lead this cycle. The freshest is Cisco's Unified CM SSRF (CVE-2026-20230, CVSS 8.6) with public proof-of-concept code — patch or disable WebDialer now. Two flaws are under active exploitation: a WordPress Kirki account-takeover bug (CVE-2026-8206, CVSS 9.8) and an Oracle WebLogic flaw (CVE-2024-21182) now on CISA's KEV list. CISA also warns of attacks on internet-exposed fuel-tank gauges, a real operational-technology risk, while a Chinese-speaking group spreads the new Atlas RAT and an AI tool surfaced a two-year-old Redis RCE (CVE-2026-23479). Patch Cisco, Kirki, WebLogic and Redis; pull tank gauges offline.

stories.headline: Cisco warns of critical Unified CM flaw with public PoC exploit code
stories.summary: Cisco disclosed a server-side request forgery flaw in Unified Communications Manager (and Unified CM Session Management Edition) that lets an unauthenticated remote attacker write arbitrary files to the underlying OS via crafted HTTP requests to the WebDialer service, enabling later root privilege escalation. Cisco rates it a Critical security advisory and confirms public proof-of-concept exploit code exists, though it has not yet seen active exploitation; NVD scores it CVSS 8.6 (High). WebDialer is disabled by default; fixes ship in 14SU6 and 15SU5.

stories.headline: Chinese-speaking group TA4922 deploys new Atlas RAT in global phishing campaigns
stories.summary: Proofpoint attributes a surge of phishing campaigns to TA4922 (overlapping with activity tracked as Silver Fox / Void Arachne), now running more unique campaigns than any other cybercrime actor it follows. The group uses localised payroll, tax-audit and government-compliance lures to deliver the new Atlas RAT alongside loaders such as RomulusLoader and Winos4.0/ValleyRAT, targeting Germany, Italy, the UK, South Africa and East Asia. Researchers note signs of AI-assisted malware development. The campaign attribution is vendor research, not a confirmed CVE.

stories.headline: Autonomous AI tool finds two-year-old RCE flaw in Redis (CVE-2026-23479)
stories.summary: A use-after-free in Redis blocking-client code (unblockClientOnKey in blocked.c), introduced in Redis 7.2.0 and unnoticed for two years, lets an authenticated user run arbitrary OS commands on the host. NVD rates it CVSS 8.8 (3.1); Redis lists 7.7 (CVSS 4.0). It was notably discovered by an autonomous AI bug-hunting tool. Many cloud Redis instances run without a password, where the default user already holds the needed privileges. Patched on 5 May (7.2.14, 7.4.9, 8.2.6, 8.4.3, 8.6.3); operators should update and require authentication.

stories.headline: CISA warns of active attacks targeting internet-exposed fuel tank monitoring systems
stories.summary: CISA and federal partners warned that automatic tank gauge (ATG) systems exposed to the internet are being attacked across the energy, chemical, food/agriculture and transportation sectors. Attackers exploit authentication-bypass flaws, hardcoded credentials, OS command execution, SQL injection and privilege escalation to alter settings and potentially disable safety monitoring. There is no formal attribution, though some reporting points to Iranian actors. Recommended mitigations: remove ATGs from the internet, enforce multi-factor authentication, and place devices behind firewalls or VPNs.

stories.headline: Critical Kirki WordPress plugin flaw exploited to hijack admin accounts
stories.summary: An unauthenticated privilege-escalation flaw in the Kirki WordPress plugin (versions 6.0.0–6.0.6) exposes a custom password-reset REST endpoint, letting attackers take over any account — including administrators — with a single crafted HTTP request. Wordfence/Defiant observed active exploitation, reporting more than 220 blocked attempts in 24 hours; roughly 150,000 of the plugin's 500,000-plus installs may be exposed. The issue is fixed in version 6.0.7, which site owners should apply immediately.

stories.headline: CISA adds actively exploited Oracle WebLogic flaw (CVE-2024-21182) to its KEV catalog
stories.summary: CISA added a 2024 Oracle WebLogic Server flaw to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog on 1 June after confirming active exploitation, setting a federal remediation deadline of 4 June. The bug lets an unauthenticated attacker with network access compromise the server via the T3 and IIOP protocols. Oracle patched it in July 2024; exact in-the-wild mechanics are not yet public. Organisations running WebLogic should verify they are patched and restrict T3/IIOP access.
